title: Modularity

content.question: What in your system is tangled together that would work better separated?
sop: Map your current system end-to-end and mark every point where two functions depend on each other to run.
Rate how tangled each dependency is 1-10, how much breaking one part would break the other (see calculation, page 4)
Pick the most tangled pair and separate it into two standalone modules with a clear handoff between them.
Give each module its own owner, process, or tool so it can improve without touching the others.
Re-test: change one module and confirm the rest keeps running untouched.
questions: What in your system is tangled together that would work better separated?
If you had to hand off just one part of your business tomorrow, could someone run it without understanding the rest?
Which two unrelated tasks have you bundled together out of habit, not necessity?
content.equation_example: Independent Modules equals 3 (count of parts that can change without breaking the others). Flexibility equals 3. Higher means more of your system can be improved, replaced, or handed off in isolation. Lower (Independent Modules equals 1) means everything is fused together, one change risks breaking the whole system.
content.closing_statement: Separable parts move faster than fused ones.

caption:
Principle Nº 127: Modularity

Systems should be separable.

Ask yourself: What in your system is tangled together that would work better separated?
